coredata.dc:title: Electrodeposition of ZnO nanoflake-based photoanode sensitized by carbon quantum dots for photoelectrochemical water oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Changhai
Qiu, Yangyang
Wang, Fang
Li, Longzhu
Liang, Qian
Chen, Zhidong
coredata.dc:description: We report a promising simple strategy for improving the performance of the photoanode for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation. Three-dimentional hierarchical ZnO nanoflake arrays with abundant porosity and small thickness on fluorine-doped tin oxide glass substrate (FTO) was prepared with electrodeposition. The ZnO nanoflake-based photoanode exhibits superior photoresponse and PEC capability. Furthermore, the ZnO photoanode sensitized by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can further PEC performance due to the narrower bandgap of CQDs and the improved efficiency of photogenerated electrons transfer from CQDs to ZnO nanostructures. The morphology and properties of the sample were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), cross-section SEM, UV–vis spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), FT-IR, X-ray diffractometry (XRD) and electrical measurements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrodeposition
ZnO nanoflake
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Photoelectrochemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The SEM images of ZnO photoanodes from Zn(NO3)2 concentration of (a, b) 0.01mol/L, (c, d) 0.05mol/L, and (e, f) 0.15mol/L. The scale bar for left is 5µm and for right is 0.5µm.

The cross-section SEM images of ZnO photoanodes from Zn(NO3)2 concentration of (a) 0.01mol/L, (b) 0.05mol/L, and (c) 0.15mol/L; (d) XPS survey, (e) Zn 2p XPS and (f) UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnO_0.15 and the corresponding ZnO/CQDs samples.

(a) XRD patterns of ZnO/CQDs composites prepared from different Zn(NO3)2 concentration and the standard XRD patterns of hexagonal wurtzite structures of ZnO (JCPDS, No.05-0664) and (b) FTIR spectra of ZnO_0.15 and corresponding ZnO/CQDs.

Linear sweep voltammetry curves of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs hetrojunction photoanodes from Zn(NO3)2 concentration of (a) 0.01mol/L, (b) 0.05mol/L, and (c) 0.15mol/L, under light or dark conditions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure ,the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Chronoamperometric curves of ZnO films from 0.01, 0.05, and 0.15mol/L, and ZnO/CQDs heterojunction photoanode from 0.15mol/L at an applied bias of 1.23 VRHE; (b) Transient OCP of ZnO films from 0.15mol/L and 0.01mol/L in dark and under light illumination.

head.ce:title: Electrodeposition of ZnO nanoflake-based photoanode sensitized by carbon quantum dots for photoelectrochemical water oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Material Surface Technology, Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Photovoltaic Science and Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213164, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Material Surface Technology, Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Photovoltaic Science and Engineering, Changzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a promising simple strategy for improving the performance of the photoanode for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation. Three-dimentional hierarchical ZnO nanoflake arrays with abundant porosity and small thickness on fluorine-doped tin oxide glass substrate (FTO) was prepared with electrodeposition. The ZnO nanoflake-based photoanode exhibits superior photoresponse and PEC capability. Furthermore, the ZnO photoanode sensitized by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can further PEC performance due to the narrower bandgap of CQDs and the improved efficiency of photogenerated electrons transfer from CQDs to ZnO nanostructures. The morphology and properties of the sample were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), cross-section SEM, UV–vis spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), FT-IR, X-ray diffractometry (XRD) and electrical measurements.

Three-dimentional hierarchical ZnO nanoflake arrays with abundant porosity and small thickness sensitized with CQDs were prepared for highly efficient PEC water oxidation.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: The inexpensive earth abundant materials need to be developed as the semiconductors, catalysts, or conducting electrodes for renewable energy technologies, in order to meet the demands of scale and significance of the energy challenges. However, there are many shortcomings in applications for most abundant materials, due to the inferior properties and performance in comparison with their more conventional peers [1]. Zinc oxide, as an environmentally friendly, transparent n-type oxide semiconductor with a direct band gap (3.37eV at room temperature) [2–4], has drawn great attention in various applications such as varistors, phosphors, solar cells, solid-state display, devices and sensors, owing to its ease of fabrication, low cost and non-toxicity [5–9].

Aligned ZnO arrays show superior advantages for photoelectrochemical (PEC) applications compared with TiO2-based dye-sensitized solar cells, because it is difficult to fabricate vertical single-crystalline TiO2 NWs on the transparent conducting oxide substrate [10]. However, the wide bandgap greatly limits the use of visible light. Numerous innovative methods have been employed as highly effective routes to improve the products’ ability to use visible light and consequently achieve greater photocatalytic capacity than pure ZnO nanocrystals, such as surface modification with metals (Eu, Au, and Ag) or nonmetals (C, N, and P), heterojunctions (CdS, ZnS, C3N4, and Ag2S), increasing the surface area, and generating defect sites [5,7,9,11–17].

In this paper, the sensitized ZnO-based photoanodes by carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by impregnation of CQDs atop ZnO nanostructure with the purpose of increasing the charge separation efficiency. CQDs exhibit many noticeable advantages compared to rare-earth doped materials such as low cost, easy fabrication, abundance in the earth, and excellent optical properties [18–20]. With the adjustable energy level structure of CQDs, the heterojunction of ZnO/CQDs can improve the efficiency of photogenerated electrons transfer from CQDs to ZnO nanostructures, thereby leading to reduced anodic corrosion/decomposition and greatly improved stability of photodevices [7]. Meanwhile, CQDs can supply more excited electrons under illumination with light, due to the narrow bandgap of CQDs compared with ZnO. In addition, three-dimentional hierarchical ZnO nanoflake arrays with abundant porosity and small thickness were prepared with electrodeposition [19,21,22]. The as-prepared ZnO nanoflake arrays photoanode are expected to contribute to the improvement of charge separation efficiency and PEC performance.

All the reagent used in this research are analytical grade, which was used without further purification. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), ammonium citrate, and hexamethylenetetramine (HMT) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Potassium chloride (KCl) and ethylenediamine were purchased from Shanghai Ling Feng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: Before the film deposition, the FTO substrates were cleaned in sequence with acetone, ethanol, and deionized (DI) water in ultrasonic bath and then dried under N2 flow. ZnO films were produced by eletrodeposition at 75℃ from Zn(NO3)2 aqueous solution with supporting electrolyte of 0.1M KCl. The concentration of Zn(NO3)2 was 0.01, 0.05 and 0.15mol/L, and the products was labeled as ZnO_0.01, ZnO_0.05 and ZnO_0.15, respectively. HMT was used as a pH buffer to adjust pH value around 6. Three electrode cell was used for film deposition with FTO glass as the working electrode with an effective deposition area of 1cm2. A platinum rod was used as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl saturated electrode was used as reference eletrode. Electrodepositions were carried out potentio statically at −0.85V (versus Ag/AgCl) for 30min.

CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method. Ammonium citrate (16mmol) and ethyenediamine (4mL) were dissolved in water (20mL). Then the solution was transferred to a Teflonlined autoclave (100mL) and kept at 200℃ for 5h. The collected solution was dialyzed for 48h. The previously prepared ZnO films was immersed in the CQDs solution for 6h to fabricate CQDs-modified ZnO photoanode, and then followed by calcination in air at 90℃ for 1h.

The PEC properties of the fabricated samples were measured by electrochemical workstation (CHI760E) with three electrode cell system in an aqueous solution of 0.1M Na2SO4 as electrolyte. Light was provided by a 300W Xe arc lamp fitted with an AM 1.5G filter, and the power density was adjusted to 100mW/cm2. In our paper, all the potential readings were reported with respect to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) by: ERHE= EAg/AgCl+0.059*5.6+0.197= EAg/AgCl + 0.53 (V). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were collected at a scan rate of 10mV/s with or without illumination.

The microstructure of ZnO was characterized with scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 1. Uniform ZnO nanorods (ZnO NRs) are presented with the diameters around 400–500nm in Figs. 1a and 1b, obtained from precursor concentration of 0.01mol/L. With the concentration of Zn(NO3)2 increasing to 0.05mol/L, the diameters of asymmetric ZnO nanorods in Figs. 1c and 1d obviously grow to 750–1169nm. However, the final product from Zn(NO3)2 concentration of 0.15mol/L is entirely comprised of dense, vertically aligned ZnO nanoflakes (ZnO NFs) arrays grown on FTO over large areas, as shown in Figs. 1e and 1f, with the thickness of nanoflakes dramatically decreased to 123–248nm.

As shown in Figs. 2a to 2c, the cross-section SEM was used to measure the thickness of ZnO nanorods arrays and hierarchical 3-dimentional ZnO nanoflakes electrodeposited on the surface of FTO substrate. From the images we can see that the thickness of ZnO increase from about 688 to 2000nm with the increasing concentration of Zn(NO3)2 from 0.01 to 0.15mol/L. X-ray photoelectron spectra were carried out to determine the components and the interaction between ZnO and CQDs. From Fig. 2d we can see that the film are composed with elements Zn, O, C and little N. The Zn and O signals are from ZnO nanoflake and C and N peaks are attributed to the adsorbed CQDs on the surface of ZnO film. Because ammonium citrate was used in the process of CQDs synthesis, there are little nitrogen contained in the CQDs. Due to electron-rich properties for CQDs, the zinc atoms in ZnO will get some electrons from CQDs, so the binding energy (BE) of Zn 2p will shift negatively to the lower BE, presented in the Fig. 2e. In addition, with the addition of CQDs, the light absorption in the UV and visible region is greatly improved as shown in Fig. 2f, attributed to the much narrower bandgap of absorbed CQDs on the ZnO films, which is significantly beneficial to the enhance the utilization of light and the PEC performance of ZnO photoanode.

Fig. 3a shows the XRD patterns of electrodeposited ZnO/CQDs films with different morphologies. All of XRD patterns can be indexed as hexagonal wurtzite structures of ZnO, matched with the standard XRD patterns (JCPDS, No. 05–0664). And the peaks of CQDs cannot be observed in XRD patterns of the composites, indicating that the modified CQDs would not change the ZnO crystal lattice and the little CQDs in these composites cannot be detected. With the diameter of nanorods increase from ZnO_0.01 to ZnO_0.05, a substantially higher intensity is observed for the (002) diffraction peaks, indicating that the ZnO crystallites are oriented perfectly with their c-axis being perpendicular to the FTO substrate. Furthermore, the XRD patterns for ZnO_0.15 presents a preferential (101) and (002) orientation, with slightly increase for the intensities of (100) compared with that of ZnO_0.01 and ZnO_0.05, indicating the formation of ZnO nanoflakes arrays [23].

FT-IR spectra were carried out to confirm the existence of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 3b. Looking at the spectra of ZnO_0.15 and ZnO nanoflakes sensitized with CQDs, there are several characteristic absorption bands for ZnO/CQDs positioned at ~3400 (O–H stretching), ~2925 and 2853 (–CH3 and –CH2 stretching), ~1648 and 1548 (C˭O and –NH2), which are functional groups from CQDs prepared from ammonium citrate, indicating the presentence of CQDs for ZnO photoanodes immersed in CQDs solutions.

We applied the ZnO/CQDs nanostructure to a PEC cell as a photoanode for water splitting. For PEC performance test, a 3-electrode PEC system was operated under the 100mW/cm2 AM 1.5G illumination form backside with 0.5M Na2SO4 as electrolyte, which consisted ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanostructure on FTO glass as a working electrode, a Pt rods as a counter electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode. The photocurrent is arising from the photoexcited electrons generated and transported to the Pt counter electrode, when the light is illuminated on the photoanode. The ZnO photoanode displays obvious anodic response with an onset potential of ~0.3V versus reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) as shown in Fig. 4. For different ZnO photoanode, the photocurrent gets larger with the increasing concentration of Zn(NO3)2, due to the increase of effective photic area with the larger thickness and diameter of nanodods. Especially, the ZnO nanoflake photoanode exhibits the largest photocurrent density, ~0.35mA/cm2 at the thermodynamic potential of water oxidation (1.23 VRHE). It is deduced that the superior PEC performance for ZnO nanoflake photoanode is attributed to the three-dimentional hierarchical arrays and abundant porosity and small thickness of ZnO nanoflakes, which can increase the effective area of shining light.

In an attempt to further increase the charge separation efficiency, we designed ZnO/CQDs heterojunction by impregnation of CQDs atop ZnO nanorods and nanoflake arrays. From the blue curves in Fig. 4 for different ZnO photoanode we can see that, the photocurrent of ZnO/CQDs photoanodes will dramatically enhanced compared to the corresponding ZnO photoanodes. What is more important is that the photocurrent for ZnO/CQDs from ZnO nanoflakes arrays can increase to 0.54mA/cm2. So with the assist of CQDs, the charge separation gets higher efficiency and the photocorrosion of ZnO weakens in the heterojunction photoanode, consistent with the UV–vis results of Fig. 2f.

To examine the photoresponse of ZnO-based photoanode over time, the chronoamperometric curves at the thermodynamic potential of water oxidation (1.23 1.23 VRHE) was measured ( Fig. 5). A spike in the photoresponse is observed upon illumination with light, owing to the transient effect in power excitation. Meanwhile, the photocurrent then quickly returned to a steady state. In the long term, the photocurrent response is almost identical, demonstrating very good stability of ZnO-based photoanodes. The achieved high efficiency and stability can be attributed to two major improvement. First, the heterojunction between ZnO and CQDs allows fast and efficiency transfer of the photogenerated electrons from CQDs to ZnO nanostructures, leading to reduced anodic corrosion/decomposition and greatly improved stability of photodevices. Second, the CQDs supply multiple excitons from single photon upon the illumination with light [18].

To investigate the injection direction of photogenerated electrons under illumination with light, the open circuit potential (OCP) transient tests of the prepared ZnO nanorods and nanoflakes photoanodes were carried out and the results were shown in Fig. 5b. It can be seen that ZnO_0.01 and ZnO_0.15 samples both show a negative increase in voltage under light irradiation, indicating that the photogenerated electrons are injected from the ZnO films into FTO substrate, resulting in the formation of anodic photocurrent in I-V and I-t measurements (Figs. 4 and 5a). It is noted that the difference between the voltage in dark and light on of ZnO_0.15 nanoflakes sample (0.240V) is larger than that of the ZnO_0.01 nanorods sample (0.179V), indicating that the prepared ZnO nanoflakes sample shows more remarkable photoelectric conversion ability, which is also consist with the results of Figs. 4 and 5a.

According to the results discussed above, we propose a possible mechanism for the improved charge separation of ZnO/CQDs hetero-structure nanorods (NRs) and nanoflakes (NFs) arrays under visible light illumination. The higher photocatalytic activity of ZnO/CQDs hetero-structure than ZnO sample implies that the electronic interaction by chelation between ZnO and CQDs plays an important roles. The chelation can not only significantly increase the separation of photogenerated charge, but also enhance the transfer of electron-hole pairs. The introduction of CQDs on the surface of ZnO can improve the photocatalytic activity of ZnO, attributed to the efficient charge separation caused by the rapid photo-induced charge transfer, and the inhibition of the recombination of electron-hole pairs, which can increase the lifetime of charge carriers and further enhance the activity of oxygen evolution on the interfacial reaction.

In summary, we reported a facile route to fabricate CQDs sensitized three-dimentional hierarchical ZnO nanoflake arrays for PEC water oxidation via electrodeposition carried out potentio statically at −0.85V. The final products were comprised of dense, vertically aligned nanoflakes with abundant porosity and small thickness. Compared to conventional ZnO polycrystal films, our nanoflake array photoanodes exhibited larger photocurrent density, ~0.35mA/cm2 at the thermodynamic potential of water oxidation. We designed ZnO/CQDs heterojunction by impregnation of CQDs atop ZnO nanoflake arrays, aimed to further increase the charge separation efficiency. The treated photoanodes produced large photocurrent density about 0.54mA/cm2. Our study proposes the significant potential of ZnO nanoflake photoanodes for PEC water oxidation and provide a new opportunity for advanced electrode materials in the application of high performance photo water oxidation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51574047), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Nos. BK20160277, BK20150259).
